paper_title: The hat guessing number of graphs
summary: Problem 1.4 remains largely open. Part (i) (HG(G) bounded by a function of maximum degree) was already settled before the problem was stated via the Lovász Local Lemma. Parts (ii) and (iii) are unresolved: for part (ii), several papers have studied hat guessing numbers of degenerate and strongly degenerate graphs and shown that HG can grow at least as fast as $2^{2^{d-1}}$ for $d$-degenerate graphs, but whether an upper bound $f_2(d)$ exists is still open; for part (iii), no evidence of a minimum-degree lower bound was found.

since_posted.title: On the Hat Guessing Number of Graphs
since_posted.claim: Restates Problem 1.4 and notes a positive answer seems plausible but does not prove it; constructs a planar graph with HG(G)=12 and analyzes random and complete multipartite graphs.

internal_refs_verified.note: Abstract confirmed via WebFetch; paper is about hat guessing numbers and constructs a planar graph with HG=12, studies random and complete multipartite graphs, but does not resolve Problem 1.4 parts (ii)-(iii).

Paper is about bipartite graph list coloring (choosability bound (4/5-epsilon)*Delta/log Delta), not hat guessing numbers; not relevant to this conjecture. The internal-ref attribution appears to be a false match.

search_queries: hat guessing number degeneracy minimum degree graph 1812.09752
hat guessing number degenerate graphs Xiaoyu He arxiv counterexample minimum degree
notes: Web search found two further highly relevant papers not verified by WebFetch due to the 5-call cap: arXiv:2003.04990 (Xiaoyu He, 'Hat Guessing Numbers of Degenerate Graphs', Electronic Journal of Combinatorics 2020) showing HG(G) >= 2^{2^{d-1}} for some d-degenerate G and giving new upper-bound methods, and arXiv:2112.09619 ('Hat guessing numbers of strongly degenerate graphs', SIAM J. Discrete Math. ~2023); search summaries indicate that the general degeneracy-bound question of part (ii) remains open as of those papers. Part (iii) (minimum-degree lower bound) has no follow-up evidence in indexed literature.
